document_text:
SPD-RAG: Sub-Agent Per Document Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Yagiz Can Akay1Muhammed Yusuf Kartal1Esra Alparslan1
Faruk Ortakoyluoglu1Arda Akpinar2
1TOBB University of Economics and Technology2OSTIM Technical University
{y.akay, m.kartal, ealparslan, fortakoyluoglu}@etu.edu.tr 230206003@ostimteknik.edu.tr
Abstract
Answering complex, real-world queries often
requires synthesizing facts scattered across vast
document corpora. In these settings, stan-
dard retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
pipelines suffer from incomplete evidence cov-
erage, while long-context large language mod-
els (LLMs) struggle to reason reliably over
massive inputs. We introduce SPD-RAG, a
hierarchical multi-agent framework for exhaus-
tive cross-document question answering that de-
composes the problem along the document axis.
Each document is processed by a dedicated
document-level agent operating only on its own
content, enabling focused retrieval, while a co-
ordinator dispatches tasks to relevant agents
and aggregates their partial answers. Agent
outputs are synthesized by merging partial an-
swers through a token-bounded synthesis layer
(which supports recursive map-reduce for mas-
sive corpora). This document-level specializa-
tion with centralized fusion improves scalabil-
ity and answer quality in heterogeneous multi-
document settings while yielding a modular,
extensible retrieval pipeline. On the LOONG
benchmark (EMNLP 2024) for long-context
multi-document QA, SPD-RAG achieves an
Avg Score of 58.1 (GPT-5 evaluation), outper-
forming Normal RAG (33.0) and Agentic RAG
(32.8) while using only 38% of the API cost of
a full-context baseline (68.0).
1 Introduction
Large language models (LLMs) and the agentic
systems built around them are increasingly used
for complex information search tasks (Guo et al.,
2024). Real-world questions often require synthe-
sizing evidence scattered across many documents,
such as assessing a company’s financial risks across
years of reports or integrating findings from mul-
tiple scientific papers.1Such questions are natural
1Examples inspired by scenarios in the Loong bench-
mark (Wang et al., 2024a).and common, yet expose critical coverage and rea-
soning bottlenecks in current NLP systems.
Traditional single-index or single-agent retrieval
systems struggle to maintain both scalability and
contextual relevance over large, heterogeneous cor-
pora, especially for long or structurally complex
documents. Standard RAG pipelines retrieve a
fixed number of documents Kand process them
within a single context window (Lewis et al., 2020),
which fails when answers depend on information
distributed across many documents, since evidence
beyond the top- Kresults is typically discarded in
a single retrieval pass. Long-context LLMs ex-
tend context windows to 128K–2M tokens (Gemini
Team et al., 2024), but empirical evidence shows
that reasoning quality degrades significantly as con-
text length increases (Liu et al., 2023). This sug-
gests that the bottleneck is not only retrieval, but
alsoreasoning at scaleover many documents and
hundreds of thousands of tokens.
In this work, we introduce SPD-RAG
(Sub-agent per Document Retrieval-Augmented
Generation), a hierarchical multi-agent architecture
forexhaustivemulti-document question answering
that factors the problem along the document axis
rather than the task axis. Instead of forcing a
single model to hunt through a massive global
index, SPD-RAG uses a central coordinator to
decompose the user’s query into shared instruc-
tions. It then deploys a dedicated, cost-efficient
sub-agent toeachdocument in the corpus. These
agents operate entirely in parallel, treating their
assigned documents as isolated retrieval universes
to extract relevant findings. Finally, a synthesis
model aggregates these document-grounded
findings—employing a recursive merging fallback
for exceptionally large corpora—to construct a
comprehensive, final answer. Our codebase and
datasets used in this paper can be found at
https://github.com/NebulAICompany/
SPD-RAG.arXiv:2603.08329v1  [cs.CL]  9 Mar 2026

Our contributions are as follows:
•We propose SPD-RAG, a hierarchical multi-
agent framework that combines per-document
agentic RAG with a centralized synthesis
layer, where cost-efficient document agents
are coordinated by a smarter coordinator
agent, enabling document-level specialization
and parallel execution while allowing each
document to be analyzed in depth to ensure
that all relevant information is incorporated
without missing critical evidence.
•We evaluate on the Loong benchmark (Wang
et al., 2024a), which includes multi-document
QA instances over financial reports and aca-
demic papers with an average of 11 docu-
ments per instance and context lengths from
10K to beyond 250K tokens, and show that
our system substantially outperforms standard
RAG and Agentic RAG baselines under GPT-
5-judged Avg Score (adapted from Loong’s
GPT-4-judged protocol; see §4.3), with gains
of around +25 points (76% higher average
score) compared to Normal RAG and Agentic
RAG baselines.
•We analyze ablations, document type and task
complexity effects, and cost–quality tradeoffs,
showing that SPD-RAG attains over 85% of
full-context baseline quality at roughly 38%
of the API cost.
2 Related Work
2.1 LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
LLM-based multi-agent systems are increasingly
used for complex task solving. Guo et al. (2024)
provide a comprehensive survey of agent profiles,
communication protocols, and collaboration strate-
gies. Hierarchical architectures such as MegaA-
gent (Wang et al., 2024b), which demonstrates au-
tonomous cooperation among up to 590 agents via
multi-level task decomposition, and AgentOrches-
tra (Zhang et al., 2025), which achieves state-of-the-
art performance on GAIA using a central planning
agent and specialized sub-agents, exemplify this
trend.
Scaling laws for multi-agent systems offer quan-
titative guidance for architecture design. Kim et al.
(2025) evaluate 180 configurations across five ar-
chitectures, finding that centralized coordination
yields an 80.9% improvement on parallelizabletasks, but that multi-agent overhead grows superlin-
early. Their result that independent agents amplify
errors by 17.2 ×compared to 4.4 ×for centralized
systems directly motivates our coordinator-based
design. The taxonomy of hierarchical multi-agent
systems by Moore (2025) further classifies design
patterns along five axes: control hierarchy, informa-
tion flow, role delegation, temporal layering, and
communication structure.
2.2 Long Document and Multi-Document QA
Long documents that exceed LLM context win-
dows are often handled via divide-and-conquer
strategies. LLM ×MapReduce (Zhou et al., 2025)
formalizes two key challenges:inter-chunk depen-
dency(one chunk depends on context from another)
andinter-chunk conflict(chunks provide contradic-
tory information), and proposes structured informa-
tion protocols and in-context confidence calibration
to mitigate them. ToM (Guo et al., 2025) extends
this approach with a hierarchical DocTree repre-
sentation for recursive reasoning.
For multi-document QA, LongAgent (Zhao et al.,
2024) splits a 128K-token document into chunks
assigned to member agents, with a leader agent
orchestrating discussions and inter-member com-
munication to reduce hallucinations. However,
LongAgent targets asinglelong document rather
thanmultiple independentdocuments that may con-
flict. DocAgent (Sun et al., 2025) uses a multi-
agent framework that mimics human reading via
a tree-structured outline and reviewer agent, while
MDocAgent (Han et al., 2025) employs five spe-
cialized agents for multi-modal document under-
standing.
2.3 Hierarchical and Recursive
Summarization
Our hierarchical merging mechanism builds on tree-
based retrieval and recursive summarization. RAP-
TOR (Sarthi et al., 2024) recursively embeds, clus-
ters, and summarizes text chunks to construct a
bottom-up tree with multiple abstraction levels; re-
trieving from the appropriate level yields a 20% ab-
solute accuracy gain on QuALITY . Ou and Lapata
(2025) show that hierarchical merging can amplify
hallucinations during recursive summarization and
propose context-aware augmentation strategies that
replace intermediate summaries with relevant input
context, which we adopt in our merging protocol.

2.4 Graph-Based Document Analysis
Graph-based document analysis is particu-
larly effective for global sensemaking queries.
GraphRAG (Edge et al., 2024) constructs a knowl-
edge graph of entities and relations from source
documents and applies hierarchical community
detection (e.g., the Leiden algorithm (Traag et al.,
2019)) to obtain multi-level communities with
textual summaries. At query time, it retrieves
relevant communities, elicits community-level
partial answers, and aggregates them via a
map–reduce–style procedure. A survey on LLM-
empowered knowledge graph construction (Bian,
2025) highlights how LLMs are reshaping the
classical knowledge graph pipeline across three
key stages: ontology engineering, knowledge
extraction, and knowledge fusion. Our work is
complementary: instead of inducing an explicit
knowledge graph, SPD-RAG operates directly
over agent-produced textual summaries and
performs similarity-guided recursive synthesis
across many documents.
2.5 Benchmarks for Multi-Document
Reasoning
The Loong benchmark (Wang et al., 2024a)
evaluates long-context LLMs on extended multi-
document QA where every document is relevant
("Leave No Document Behind") across financial,
legal, and academic domains in English and Chi-
nese. It spans four task types with context lengths
from 10K to over 250K tokens, and even strong
frontier models achieve only modest Avg Scores
and medium perfect-answer rates, underscoring the
difficulty of reasoning over many long, all-relevant
documents.
Complementary datasets probe other aspects of
multi-document reasoning. MoNaCo (Wolfson
et al., 2025) focuses on natural questions over
dozens of Wikipedia pages, MEBench (Lin et al.,
2025) on high-entity-density QA, FanOutQA (Zhu
et al., 2024) on multi-hop questions with
long cross-document evidence chains, and
HoloBench (Maekawa et al., 2024) on database-
style aggregation over unstructured text. Taken
together, these benchmarks show that scalable
multi-document reasoning remains an open
challenge, with Loong emphasizing the especially
demanding all-relevant, long-context regime.3 Methodology
Figure 1 presents an overview of the SPD-RAG
architecture. The system processes a query through
three layers: the Coordination Layer (§3.1), the
Parallel Retrieval Layer (§3.2), and the Synthesis
Layer (§3.3).
3.1 Coordination Layer
Given a query qand corpus D, the system first
processes the query through a coordinator agent.
The coordinator’s role is to decompose the user
query into aShared Instruction Set for Docu-
mentsandSynthesis Directives. It generates a
WriteTodos structured object containing (a) a list
ofsub_agent_todos : atomic, self-contained ex-
traction tasks specifying exactly what fields, en-
tities, or numeric values to extract (the shared in-
structions); and (b) a synthesis_directive (2–4
sentences) instructing the downstream Synthesis
Layer on how to prioritize and structure the merged
response.
3.2 Parallel Retrieval Layer
We assign a dedicated sub-agent αito each docu-
ment diin the corpus. Each sub-agent operates as
an independent RAG loop over its assigned docu-
ment, guided by the shared instruction set from the
Coordination Layer.
Document-Scoped Retrieval and Reasoning.
Each document diserves as an isolated retrieval
universe; the assigned sub-agent is strictly con-
strained to search tool calls within di, preventing
cross-document distractor chunks from degrading
local extraction. Each sub-agent receives the
original query q, its assigned document name, and
the shared instruction set (todo list), then operates
in an iterative retrieve-and-reason loop. On each
turn, the agent either issues a search action (a
focused query string) or a finalize action that
terminates the loop and emits the document-
grounded findings. The sub-agent prompt instructs
the agent to attempt at least 2 focused searches
before concluding a piece of information is
absent, with a total cap of 5 search calls across
all tasks. Each search action triggers dense
vector retrieval over the sub-agent’s dedicated
per-document Qdrant index. This retrieval uses Co-
hereembed-v4.0 embeddings (1536-dimensional,
cosine similarity) to fetch the top k= 15 most
similar chunks. These chunks are then re-ranked
by Cohere rerank-v4.0-fast to yield a final set

Figure 1: Overview of SPD-RAG Architecture.
oftop_n= 5 chunks per search. The documents
are split into chunks offline using LangChain’s
RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter in Mark-
down mode with a chunk size of 1000 tokens and
250-token overlap.
Findings Output.We denote the findings of each
sub-agent as
oi=αi(q, d i) =⟨s i, ri⟩(1)
where siis the natural language findings re-
port from diandri∈[0,1] is a scalar rele-
vance/confidence score. These outputs are passed
to the Synthesis Layer.
Parallelism.All RAG loops for the docu-
ments are dispatched concurrently via Lang-
Graph’s Send API fan-out, which emits one
Send("document_sub_agent_node", ...) per
document and executes all targets in parallel within
LangGraph’s async runtime.
3.3 Synthesis Layer
While modern LLMs feature extensive context win-
dows, synthesizing hundreds of document reports
can still exceed these limits or cause ’lost in the
middle’ degradation. To future-proof the architec-
ture for massive corpora, the Synthesis Layer is
designed as a dynamic map-reduce pipeline that ag-
gregates findings recursively. The Synthesis Layer
receives the synthesis directives from the Coor-
dination Layer and the findings {o1, o2, . . . , o n}
from the Parallel Retrieval Layer. It aggregates
and synthesizes the findings recursively through a
similarity-ordered process until the total findings
size falls within the target context size to produce
the final output.Similarity-Ordered Merge & Synthesis.
In iteration t, we maintain a set of find-
ings/summaries S(t)={s(t)
1, . . . , s(t)
n}. We
embed each summary using Cohere embed-v4.0
and compute a cosine-similarity matrix via
sklearn.metrics.pairwise.cosine_similarity .
This is converted to a distance matrix
Dij= 1−cos(s(t)
i, s(t)
j), which is passed to
AgglomerativeClustering withn_clusters=1 ,
linkage="average" (UPGMA), producing a
complete dendrogram over all current summaries.
We traverse the merge tree bottom-up, greedily
accumulating nodes into a batch as long as the
totaltiktoken count does not exceed the target
budget of B= 750,000 tokens. Semantically
similar summaries are grouped first, so each batch
contains the most similar available summaries
within the token limit.
Aggregate and Synthesize Findings.For each
batch, an LLM is prompted with the concatenated
findings, the synthesis directive, and the original
query qto produce an aggregated and synthesized
summary. All batch synthesis calls within an itera-
tion are issued concurrently via asyncio.gather .
The merged summaries form the input for the next
iteration S(t+1). The same SYNTHESISprompt
used at every level of the merging tree is ap-
plied even at the final level—there is no separate
synthesis-only prompt.
Context Size Condition and Output.The layer
evaluates whether the total findings size exceeds
the context size threshold (i.e., |S(t+1)|>1 ). If
the condition is true, the process loops back to the
similarity-ordered merge and synthesis step. If the

Algorithm 1Recursive Synthesis
Require:FindingsS={s 1, . . . , s n}, queryq,
synthesis directive d, context size budget B
Ensure:Final Outputs⋆
1:while|S|>1do
2:E←COHEREEMBED(S)
3:D←1−COSINESIMILARITY(E)
4:tree←AGGLOMERATIVECLUSTER(
D,linkage=“average”)
5:batches←GROUPBYTOKENS(
S,tree, B)
6:if|batches| ≥ |S|then
7:batches←[S]
8:end if

SYNTHESIZE(b, q, d)∀b∈batches)
10:end while
11:returnS[0]
condition is false (the total findings size is within
the context size, leaving a single final summary s⋆),
the process terminates and yields s⋆as the final
output. If |S(t+1)| ≥ |S(t)|(no-progress case), the
entire current level is forced into a single batch to
guarantee termination.
Algorithm 1 gives the pseudocode for the general
recursive synthesis procedure.
Behavior on Loong.In practice, with Gemini 2.5
Pro’s 1M-token context window and a budget of
B= 750,000 tokens, the combined sub-agent find-
ings for all instances in our Loong evaluation set fit
within a single batch, so the conditional check al-
lowed output after one iteration. The recursive loop
is therefore a design capability intended for much
larger corpora, where total findings size genuinely
exceeds the context size.
4 Experimental Setup
4.1 Dataset
We evaluate SPD-RAG on the Loong benchmark
(Wang et al., 2024a). Loong is an extended multi-
document QA instances in both English and Chi-
nese, with an average of 11 documents per test
case spanning two real-world scenarios: financial
reports and academic papers. Each test case is
constructed so that every document is relevant to
the final answer ("Leave No Document Behind",
meaning a correct response requires synthesizing
evidence from all provided documents), and is an-
notated under one of four task types: SpotlightLocating, Comparison, Clustering, or Chain of
Reasoning, with context lengths ranging from 10K
to beyond 250K tokens. We evaluate on theEn-
glishandSet 4 (200k-250k tokens)portion of the
benchmark only. Our evaluation set comprises 40
instances involving academic papers and 62 involv-
ing financial reports (102 instances total), with the
four task types distributed as: Spotlight Locating
(27), Comparison (15), Clustering (49), and Chain
of Reasoning (11).
4.2 Baselines
We compare against the following systems, all
backed by Gemini 2.5 Pro:
•Baseline (Full Context): All documents for
each instance are concatenated and provided
directly in the context window of Gemini 2.5
Pro, representing the oracle long-context up-
per bound.
•Normal RAG: Standard vector search top- K
retrieval over the full document corpus, fol-
lowed by LLM reasoning over the retrieved
chunks.
•Agentic RAG: A single-agent ReAct-style
RAG system (implemented via Lang-
Graph/LangChain) that can issue multiple
iterative retrieval calls over the full global
corpus. It uses the same embedding and
re-ranking pipeline as SPD-RAG, but lacks
per-document specialization.
4.3 Evaluation Metrics
We report the following metrics, following the
Loong evaluation protocol (Wang et al., 2024a):
•Avg Score: An LLM-judged score (0–100)
measuring the degree to which the predicted
answer accurately covers the gold answer,
following the evaluation protocol of Loong
(Wang et al., 2024a).2
•Perfect Rate (PR%): Percentage of queries
where the system receives a perfect score of
100.
2Because the original legacy GPT-4 model API is unavail-
able for new scaled evaluations, we adopt GPT-5 as the eval-
uator. Recent literature increasingly adopts GPT-5 as the
standard for LLM-as-a-judge protocols due to its superior
alignment with human grading (Wolfson et al., 2025). While
our baseline numbers are re-computed under this GPT-5 judge
for strict fairness, raw scores may not be perfectly 1-to-1 com-
parable with the originally published Loong leaderboard.

•Avg Token Usage: Mean per-query total (in-
put + output) token usage.
•Avg Cost (USD): Mean per-query API cost,
enabling cost-efficiency comparisons.
•Avg Latency (s): Mean per-query wall-clock
time.
4.4 Implementation Details
All systems useGemini 2.5 Pro(temperature 0.0)
as the backbone LLM for the coordinator, merging
layer, and synthesis layer. SPD-RAG document
sub-agents useGemini 2.5 Flashfor document-
scoped retrieval, reducing per-document cost while
preserving reasoning quality at the merging and
synthesis stages. GPT-5 is used exclusively as the
LLM judge for the evaluation of the Avg score and
is not part of the pipeline itself. Prompts for all
systems, including baselines, were optimized for
information extraction and synthesis to ensure fair
comparison. Full prompt templates for all systems
are provided in the code repository.
Indexing.Each document is pre-
indexed into a dedicated Qdrant vec-
tor collection using LangChain’s
RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter (Imple-
mentation details are provided in the Appendix.).
Retrieval per sub-agent.Each search tool call
performs dense vector retrieval over the docu-
ment index, returning the top- K(k= 15 ) chunks
using cosine similarity in Qdrant. These can-
didates are subsequently re-ranked with Cohere
rerank-v4.0-fast , and the top top_n= 5 chunks
are passed to the sub-agent LLM.
Merging layer.The token budget per merge
batch is B= 750,000 tokens (75% of Gemini 2.5
Pro’s 1M context cap). Agglomerative clustering
usessklearn with UPGMA linkage and precom-
puted cosine-distance matrix.
Parallelism.Document agents run concurrently
via LangGraph Send API fan-out. Batch synthe-
sis calls within each merge iteration are paral-
lelized with asyncio.gather . Synchronous Co-
here API calls are offloaded to the thread pool via
asyncio.to_thread.
Safety limits.Each document sub-agent’s iter-
ative retrieval loop is capped at 5 iterations; the
Agentic RAG baseline caps at 10 iterations.Cost tracking.Per-query costs are computed
from LangSmith run metadata (total token counts
×per-token pricing.
5 Results
5.1 Main Results
Table 1 reports the main results across all systems
on our Loong evaluation set. Figure 2 illustrates
these performance differences across the four spe-
cific task types.
SPD-RAG achieves an Avg Score of 58.1, sub-
stantially outperforming both Normal RAG (33.0)
and Agentic RAG (32.8). This corresponds to
roughly a 25-point absolute improvement (about
76% relative gain) over standard RAG baselines.
Furthermore, SPD-RAG more than doubles the
Perfect Rate (PR) of Agentic RAG (18.6% vs.
8.8%), indicating that exhaustive document-level
processing more frequently captures the complete
set of required facts. Notably, the Agentic RAG
baseline, despite consuming ∼3×more tokens than
Normal RAG, does not yield score improvements,
suggesting that uninstructed iterative retrieval with-
out per-document specialization does not effec-
tively address the coverage challenge in exhaustive
multi-document settings. While the full-context
baseline attains the highest quality with an Avg
Score of 68.0, SPD-RAG reaches 85.4% of this
performance while consuming only 37.9% of the
API cost, demonstrating a highly favorable cost-
quality trade-off. The full-context baseline pro-
vides a strong upper bound that benefits from all
documents in one pass. Despite this, SPD-RAG
narrows the gap to 9.9 points while costing less.
5.2 Analysis by Task Type
Table 2 breaks down performance by Loong’s four
task types.
The task-type breakdown reveals a clear pattern.
For Spotlight Locating—which requires finding
a single salient fact across documents—all RAG-
based systems perform reasonably well (69–74),
while the full-context baseline leads (86.6). The
largest gaps between SPD-RAG and the RAG
baselines appear in Clustering (+40.5 pts over Nor-
mal RAG) and Chain of Reasoning (+26.2 pts over
Agentic RAG), tasks that require aggregating or
reasoning over evidence frommanydocuments si-
multaneously. On the other hand, it nearly matches
the full-context baseline (42.2 vs. 42.7) on Com-
parison tasks.

Comparison, Clustering, and Chain of Reasoning).
5.3 Analysis by Document Domain
As shown in Table 3 and Figure 3 the domain
breakdown exposes a striking failure mode of stan-
dard RAG: both Normal RAG and Agentic RAG
achieve 0% PR on academic paper instances (Avg
Scores of 15.2 and 16.8, respectively). Academic
paper instances in Loong tend to involve long, tech-
nical documents with distributed evidence, making
top-Kretrieval especially susceptible to coverage
failures. SPD-RAG dramatically recovers on this
domain (60.0 Avg Score), closing most of the gap
with the full-context baseline (78.8). On finan-
cial report instances—which tend to be shorter and
more structured—all RAG-based systems perform
better, and the gap between SPD-RAG (56.9) and
the baseline (61.0) is smaller.

ing the Baseline (Full Context), Normal RAG, Agentic

5.4 Cost–Quality Tradeoff
Figure 4: Cost–Quality tradeoff. The scatter plot illus-
trates the Pareto frontier for multi-document QA.
SPD-RAG outperforms the RAG baselines in
the cost–quality trade-off. It achieves a76%
higher average scorethan Normal RAG (58.1 vs.
33.0) while increasing the per-query cost by only
$0.023 (Table 4). In contrast, Agentic RAG is
Pareto-dominated, as it incurs higher cost than
Normal RAG ($0.098 vs. $0.080) while delivering
nearly identical quality (32.8 vs. 33.0). Compared
to the full-context baseline, which achieves an Avg
Score of68.0, SPD-RAG reaches85.4% of the
quality at only 37.9% of the cost, resulting in a
2.25×improvement in cost–quality efficiency.Crucially, the architecture of SPD-RAG enables
this cost efficiency: by constraining retrieval to
isolated, single-document spaces, we can reliably
offload the iterative reasoning to a cheaper model
(Gemini 2.5 Flash). In contrast, global iterative
baselines require the reasoning capacity of a fron-
tier model (Gemini 2.5 Pro) to navigate the fully
concatenated corpus, driving up costs.
6 Discussion
Where SPD-RAG excels.The task-type results
in Table 2 confirm our core hypothesis: architec-
tural decomposition along the document axis yields
the largest gains precisely for tasks that require
exhaustive multi-document synthesis. Clustering
tasks (+40.5 over Normal RAG) and Chain of Rea-
soning tasks (+26.2 over Agentic RAG) both de-
mand that the system form a coherent answer from
evidence distributed across all relevant documents—
exactly the scenario for which per-document agents
are designed. For Loong’s Comparison questions,
the answer is primarily determined by reading out
a small set of comparable numeric attributes from
each report and aggregating them, so once docu-
ment agents have extracted those fields, the syn-
thesizer is effectively operating over a structured
table, which helps explain why SPD-RAG’s score
on Comparison tasks (42.2) nearly matches the full-
context baseline (42.7).
Remaining gap with the full-context baseline.
Despite strong gains over RAG baselines, SPD-
RAG lags the full-context baseline by 9.9 Avg
Score points overall. Three factors likely con-
tribute: (1) Gemini 2.5 Flash, used for document
agents due to its cost efficiency, may provide
weaker agentic reasoning capabilities compared
to larger models. (2) The coordinator’s sub-task
generation may under-specify queries for highly
technical academic content, leading to incom-
plete information extraction at the document-agent
level. (3) The maximum context length observed
in Loong queries was approximately 250k tokens—

only about 25% of the 1M-token context window
of Gemini 2.5 Pro—potentially limiting the pres-
sure on the full-context baseline. Since SPD-RAG
is designed to operate on substantially larger con-
texts, its advantages may become more pronounced
under more extreme long-context conditions.
RAG baseline failure on papers.The 0% PR
rate and 15.2-16.8% Avg Score of both Normal
RAG and Agentic RAG on academic paper in-
stances highlights a systematic failure of top- K
retrieval under dense, distributed evidence. Aca-
demic papers in Loong often require synthesiz-
ing facts spread across abstracts, methods, results,
and appendices of multiple documents—a regime
where exhaustive per-document coverage proves
highly effective. The recovery by SPD-RAG to
60.0 Avg Score / 7.5% PR in this domain demon-
strates the practical value of per-document special-
ization.
Latency overhead.SPD-RAG incurs a modest
latency overhead (54.8 s vs. 40.6–45.6 s for the
baselines), primarily due to its multi-agent archi-
tecture. Although document agents run in paral-
lel, end-to-end latency is still gated by the layered
pipeline, which requires at least three sequential
LLM calls (coordination, per-document retrieval,
and synthesis) rather than a single pass.
Figure 5: Average per-query latency across systems.
7 Limitations and Future Work
SPD-RAG incurs higher per-query LLM call
counts than single-pass systems—one call per doc-
ument agent plus a final synthesis step—which
increases total token usage compared to standard
RAG baselines, though it remains at roughly one-
third the cost of the full-context baseline ($0.10 vs.
$0.27 per query) due to the use of Gemini 2.5 Flash
for document sub-agents. System quality dependson the coordinator’s ability to generate comprehen-
sive sub-tasks: under-specified tasks lead to incom-
plete document-level extractions, as observed on
technical academic paper instances. Our evaluation
is limited to two domains (academic papers and fi-
nancial reports) from the Loong benchmark; legal,
medical, or enterprise document corpora may have
different structural characteristics that alter relative
performance.
The synthesis layer of SPD-RAG is designed to
handle corpora where the combined sub-agent out-
puts exceed the model’s context window, triggering
multi-round similarity-based clustering and recur-
sive synthesis. In our Loong experiments, how-
ever, Gemini 2.5 Pro’s 1M-token context was suffi-
cient to process all sub-agent summaries in a single
merge step, so this recursive path was never acti-
vated. As a result, the scalability properties of the
recursive synthesis pipeline—its impact on answer
quality, redundancy reduction, and cost as docu-
ment counts grow into the hundreds or thousands—
remain empirically unvalidated. Evaluating SPD-
RAG on much larger corpora, and ideally on a new
benchmark specifically targeting hundreds to thou-
sands of documents per query instance, is an im-
portant direction for future work that would more
directly test the per-document agent design and the
intended large-database regime.
8 Conclusion
We presented SPD-RAG, a hierarchical multi-
agent framework designed for exhaustive multi-
document question answering. By assigning a ded-
icated agentic RAG module to each document and
merging their findings through a similarity-ordered,
token-bounded tree, our system effectively miti-
gates the limitations of both traditional top- Kre-
trieval (incomplete coverage) and isolated long-
context processing (degraded reasoning at scale).
Experiments on the challenging Loong benchmark
demonstrate that SPD-RAG achieves an average
score of 58.1, outperforming Normal RAG (33.0)
and Agentic RAG (32.8) by approximately 25 ab-
solute points (76% higher average score), while
operating at only 37.9% of the API cost of the
oracle full-context baseline (68.0). These per-
formance gains are most pronounced in tasks re-
quiring deep cross-document synthesis—such as
Clustering (+40.5 points) and Chain of Reasoning
(+26.2 points)—as well as on dense academic pa-
pers where standard RAG methods entirely fail.

Ultimately, our findings demonstrate that for
complex information-seeking queries over large
corpora,howinformation is processed is crucial.
Specifically, ensuring that each document receives
exhaustive, dedicated agentic attention proves to be
a more effective, cost-efficient, and scalable strat-
egy than simply expandinghow muchraw context
a single model can consume in one pass.
9 Ethics Statement
SPD-RAG is a retrieval-augmented generation
framework intended to improve access to infor-
mation distributed across large document collec-
tions. The system relies on commercial LLM APIs
(Gemini 2.5, Cohere), and as with any LLM-based
pipeline, outputs may contain factual errors and
should not be treated as authoritative without hu-
man verification. The increased number of API
calls per query relative to single-pass systems re-
sults in higher energy consumption, a cost that
should be weighed against the accuracy gains in
deployment decisions. Our benchmark evaluations
are conducted on publicly available datasets and
involve no human subjects or personal data. Dur-
ing the preparation of this manuscript, AI-assisted
writing tools were used to help refine language and
improve clarity. All research design, experiments,
analysis, and final decisions regarding the content
were conducted and verified by the authors.

A Prompts
All prompts are defined in
backend/core/prompts.py.
A.1 Coordinator Prompt
(LEAD_RESEARCHER_PROMPT)
You are a lead researcher coordinating
a RAG-based analysis to answer a user
query.
Pipeline Architecture:
1. YOU (now) — Decompose the query intoextraction tasks and write a synthesis
directive to guide the downstream
synthesizer.
2. Sub-agents (next) — Each document
is assigned one worker that runs your
tasks independently. Workers search
their document and report raw findings.
They run in parallel and cannot see each
other’s results.
3. Synthesizer (last) — A downstream
agent receives ALL worker findings
and merges them into a single
coherent response. It follows your
synthesis_directive to decide what to
prioritize and how to structure the
output.
You CANNOT see the names, types, or
content of the documents.
Your ONLY job in this step:
•Produce a list of subagent_todos
(as structured output): precise
extraction tasks that will be
executed independently against EACH
document by the sub-agents.
•Produce a synthesis_directive: a
concise instruction (2–4 sentences)
for the synthesizer.
Todo-writing rules:
•Decompose the user query into
concrete information requirements.
•Each todo must be self-contained
and unambiguous.
•Prefer atomic tasks over broad
tasks.
•Include coverage for: definitions,
numeric values, constraints, edge
cases, error modes, and any
explicit recommendations required
by the user query.
•Design a robust extraction list
that works for ANY document in the
set.
Important Constraints:
•Do not assume any document contains
the answer. Write todos that can
be answered with either “Found” or
“Not found in this document.”
•Tell the worker WHAT to extract, not
HOW to extract it.
•Do not synthesize, summarize, or
attempt to answer the user query
yourself.
A.2 Document Sub-Agent Prompt
(RESEARCH_SYSTEM_PROMPT)
You are a sub-agent investigating a
single document: {file_name}.
You operate inside an iterative
retrieval loop. On each turn you output
exactly one structured action:
•action="search": Issue a focused
query to retrieve information from

the document. Set query to a
specific, targeted search string.
Set reasoning to why this query is
needed.
•action="finalize": You have
gathered sufficient evidence to
address ALL assigned tasks. Set
findings to your complete extracted
report. Set reasoning to a brief
summary of what you found.
Investigation principles:
1. Start by identifying the key facts
required for each task.
2. Issue ONE focused query per turn
— prefer specific terms over broad
phrases.
3. Expand to synonyms or paraphrases
if initial results are incomplete.
4. Issue separate queries for
different aspects of a task.
5. Keep searching until every task is
covered with concrete evidence or
confirmed absent.
6. Do NOT attempt to answer the user
query directly — extract raw facts
only.
7. Do NOT finalize a task as ’Not
found’ after only a single focused
search. At least attempt 2
focused searches before concluding
information is absent.
8. Do NOT focus on very specific terms,
but rather on the general context
of the task. Always try to find the
most relevant information.
9. You must use AT MOST 5 searches to
answer a task. DO NOT OVERUSE THE
SEARCH TOOL.
Finalize report format: For each
assigned task, output either “Found:
[exact answer with supporting evidence]”
or “Not found in this document.” Always
report exact numbers, names, and dates.
Never approximate or infer beyond the
document.
A.3 Merge / Synthesis Prompt
(SYNTHESIS_PROMPT)
This prompt is used uniformly ateverylevel of the
recursive merging tree, including the final synthesis
step. There is no separate final-answer prompt.
You are a research synthesizer. Merge
the following sub-agent findings into
one compact, information-dense response
for the user.
Query: {query }
Synthesis Directive:
{synthesis_directive}
Findings Batch: {findings }
Rules:•Follow the synthesis directive
above — it defines your main goal,
priorities, and output structure.
•Keep only information that directly
helps answer the query. Discard
tangential content.
•Preserve exact numbers, names,
dates, and caveats.
•Remove redundancy. If the same
fact appears multiple times, keep
it once.
•Do NOT invent or infer facts not
present in the findings.
•Same language as the findings
(default English if mixed).
B Implementation Details
Models.The coordinator, the merging layer,
and the synthesis layer use gemini-2.5-pro
(temperature 0.0). Document sub-agents
usegemini-2.5-flash (temperature 0.0) for
document-scoped retrieval. The LLM judge
(GPT-5) is separate and is used only for the
evaluation of the Avg score. Model constants are
defined inbackend/shared/constants.py.
Hyperparameters.
Parameter Value
Chunk size (tokens) 1000
Chunk overlap (tokens) 250
Tokenizertiktoken cl100k_base
Embedding model Cohereembed-v4.0
Embedding dimension 1536
Similarity metric Cosine
Merge token budgetB750,000 tokens
Dense retrieval top-K15
Reranker Coherererank-v4.0-fast
Reranker top-N5
Sub-agent iteration safety limit 5
Clustering algorithm Agglomerative (UPGMA)
Clustering metric Precomputed cosine distance
Cost measurement.Per-query costs
are pulled from LangSmith run metadata
(extract_langsmith_info.py ) using model-
specific per-token pricing.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses SPD-RAG, a hierarchical multi-agent framework for exhaustive cross-document question answering. It introduces a novel approach that decomposes the problem along the document axis, using a central coordinator to dispatch tasks to relevant agents and aggregate their partial answers. The framework enables document-level specialization, parallel execution, and recursive merging of partial answers, improving scalability and answer quality in heterogeneous multi-document settings. The document also evaluates SPD-RAG on the Loong benchmark, showing substantial outperformance of standard RAG and Agentic RAG baselines, and analyzes ablations, document type and task complexity effects, and cost-quality tradeoffs. Additionally, it reviews related work on LLM-based multi-agent systems, long document and multi-document QA, and hierarchical and recursive summarization, highlighting the novelty and effectiveness of the proposed SPD-RAG framework.

structure.title: Related Work
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a proposed hierarchical multi-agent framework called SPD-RAG for document analysis, which combines per-document agentic RAG with a centralized synthesis layer. The framework enables document-level specialization and parallel execution, allowing for in-depth analysis of documents without missing critical evidence. The document also evaluates the framework on the Loong benchmark, showing substantial outperformance of standard RAG and Agentic RAG baselines. Additionally, it analyzes ablations, document type and task complexity effects, and cost-quality tradeoffs, demonstrating that SPD-RAG attains high-quality results at reduced API costs. The document also reviews related work on LLM-based multi-agent systems, long document and multi-document QA, and hierarchical and recursive summarization, highlighting the trends and challenges in these areas and motivating the design of the proposed framework.

nodes.title: LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a proposed hierarchical multi-agent framework called SPD-RAG for document-level question answering tasks. The main points covered include the introduction of SPD-RAG, its evaluation on the Loong benchmark, and analysis of its performance compared to standard RAG and Agentic RAG baselines. The document also reviews related work on LLM-based multi-agent systems, long document and multi-document QA, and hierarchical and recursive summarization, highlighting the trends and challenges in these areas and how they motivated the design of SPD-RAG.

nodes.title: Hierarchical and Recursive Summarization
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a proposed hierarchical multi-agent framework called SPD-RAG for document-level question answering tasks. The main points covered include the introduction of SPD-RAG, its evaluation on the Loong benchmark, and analysis of its performance compared to standard RAG and Agentic RAG baselines. The document also reviews related work on LLM-based multi-agent systems, long document and multi-document QA, and hierarchical and recursive summarization, highlighting the trends and challenges in these areas and how they motivated the design of SPD-RAG.

nodes.title: Graph-Based Document Analysis
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses graph-based document analysis and benchmarks for multi-document reasoning. It highlights the effectiveness of graph-based methods, such as GraphRAG, in constructing knowledge graphs and applying community detection to obtain multi-level communities with textual summaries. The document also introduces the SPD-RAG architecture, which operates directly over agent-produced textual summaries and performs similarity-guided recursive synthesis across many documents. Additionally, it presents various benchmarks, including Loong, MoNaCo, MEBench, FanOutQA, and HoloBench, which evaluate long-context language models on extended multi-document QA tasks and highlight the challenges of scalable multi-document reasoning. The document also delves into the methodology of the SPD-RAG system, describing its three layers: the Coordination Layer, the Parallel Retrieval Layer, and the Synthesis Layer, which work together to process a query and generate a response.

nodes.title: Benchmarks for Multi-Document Reasoning
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses graph-based document analysis and benchmarks for multi-document reasoning. It highlights the effectiveness of graph-based methods, such as GraphRAG, in constructing knowledge graphs and applying community detection to obtain multi-level communities with textual summaries. The document also introduces the SPD-RAG architecture, which operates directly over agent-produced textual summaries and performs similarity-guided recursive synthesis across many documents. Additionally, it presents various benchmarks, including Loong, MoNaCo, MEBench, FanOutQA, and HoloBench, which evaluate the difficulty of reasoning over multiple long documents. The methodology section describes the SPD-RAG architecture, including the Coordination Layer, Parallel Retrieval Layer, and Synthesis Layer, which work together to process a query and generate a response. The Parallel Retrieval Layer assigns a dedicated sub-agent to each document, which operates independently to extract relevant information using a retrieve-and-reason loop.

The document also introduces the SPD-RAG architecture, which operates directly on textual summaries and performs similarity-guided recursive synthesis across multiple documents. Additionally, it presents various benchmarks, including Loong, MoNaCo, MEBench, FanOutQA, and HoloBench, which evaluate the difficulty of reasoning over multiple long documents. The methodology section describes the SPD-RAG architecture, including the Coordination Layer, Parallel Retrieval Layer, and Synthesis Layer, which work together to process a query and generate a response. nodes.title: Parallel Retrieval Layer
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses graph-based document analysis and multi-document reasoning, highlighting the challenges of scalable multi-document reasoning. It introduces the SPD-RAG architecture, which processes a query through three layers: the Coordination Layer, the Parallel Retrieval Layer, and the Synthesis Layer. The Coordination Layer decomposes the user query into a shared instruction set and synthesis directives, while the Parallel Retrieval Layer assigns a dedicated sub-agent to each document, operating in an iterative retrieve-and-reason loop. The Synthesis Layer aggregates and synthesizes the findings recursively through a similarity-ordered process. The document also mentions various benchmarks for multi-document reasoning, including the Loong benchmark, and discusses the use of large language models (LLMs) in knowledge graph construction and multi-document reasoning.

nodes.title: Synthesis Layer
nodes.summary: The partial document describes the architecture and functionality of the SPD-RAG system, focusing on the Synthesis Layer. The main points covered include the splitting of documents into chunks, parallel processing of these chunks, and the synthesis of findings through a dynamic map-reduce pipeline. The Synthesis Layer aggregates and synthesizes findings recursively, using a similarity-ordered process and cosine-similarity matrix to group semantically similar summaries. The layer also evaluates the total findings size against a context size threshold, looping back to the merge and synthesis step if necessary, until the final output is produced.

structure.title: Experimental Setup
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the SPD-RAG architecture, a system designed for multi-document question answering. The main points covered include the overview of the SPD-RAG architecture, the parallelism of the system, and the synthesis layer that aggregates and synthesizes findings from multiple documents. The synthesis layer uses a dynamic map-reduce pipeline and a similarity-ordered process to merge findings until the total findings size falls within the target context size. The document also describes the experimental setup, including the dataset used (Loong benchmark), baselines compared (Baseline, Normal RAG, and Agentic RAG), and evaluation metrics (Avg Score and Perfect Rate). The system is designed to handle large corpora and is evaluated on a subset of the Loong benchmark, with results showing the effectiveness of the SPD-RAG architecture.

nodes.title: Dataset
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a recursive synthesis algorithm for generating summaries from a set of findings, with a context size budget. The algorithm clusters findings, groups them into batches, and synthesizes each batch to produce a final output. The document also outlines an experimental setup for evaluating the algorithm on the Loong benchmark, including the dataset, baselines, and evaluation metrics. The Loong benchmark consists of multi-document QA instances in English and Chinese, with four task types, and the evaluation metrics include average score and perfect rate. The document also mentions the use of Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-5 as part of the evaluation protocol.

nodes.title: Baselines
nodes.summary: The partial document describes a recursive synthesis algorithm for text summarization, which takes in a set of findings, a query, a synthesis directive, and a context size budget. The algorithm clusters the findings, groups them into batches, and synthesizes the batches to produce a final output. The document also discusses the experimental setup for evaluating the algorithm, including the dataset used (Loong benchmark), the baselines compared against (Full Context, Normal RAG, and Agentic RAG), and the evaluation metrics used (Avg Score and Perfect Rate). The algorithm is designed to handle large corpora and is evaluated on a subset of the Loong benchmark, with results judged by a large language model (GPT-5).

structure.title: Results
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a recursive synthesis algorithm and its application in a question-answering system. The algorithm is designed to synthesize findings from multiple documents, and it uses a combination of clustering, retrieval, and merging techniques to achieve this goal. The document also describes an experimental setup for evaluating the algorithm, including a dataset, baselines, and evaluation metrics. The results show that the proposed algorithm, called SPD-RAG, outperforms other baselines in terms of average score and perfect rate, while also being more cost-efficient. structure.title: Discussion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the performance of a hierarchical multi-agent framework called SPD-RAG in multi-document question answering tasks. The framework assigns a dedicated agentic RAG module to each document and merges their findings through a similarity-ordered, token-bounded tree. The document presents experimental results on the Loong benchmark, comparing SPD-RAG to other systems such as Normal RAG and Agentic RAG. The results show that SPD-RAG outperforms these systems, achieving a higher average score while operating at a lower cost. The document also discusses the limitations and future work of SPD-RAG, including its latency overhead, dependence on the coordinator's ability to generate comprehensive sub-tasks, and the need for evaluation on larger corpora. The main points covered in the partial document include the architecture and performance of SPD-RAG, its cost-quality tradeoff, and its advantages and limitations in multi-document question answering tasks.

structure.title: Limitations and Future Work
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the limitations and advantages of the SPD-RAG framework, a hierarchical multi-agent approach for exhaustive multi-document question answering. It highlights the framework's ability to mitigate the limitations of traditional top-K retrieval and isolated long-context processing, and its effectiveness in tasks requiring deep cross-document synthesis. The document also discusses the latency overhead, limitations, and future work directions for the SPD-RAG framework, including its evaluation on larger corpora and its scalability properties. Additionally, it presents experimental results demonstrating the framework's performance gains over baseline methods, particularly in tasks requiring cross-document synthesis and on dense academic papers.

structure.title: Conclusion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the development and evaluation of SPD-RAG, a hierarchical multi-agent framework designed for exhaustive multi-document question answering. The system assigns a dedicated agentic RAG module to each document and merges their findings through a similarity-ordered, token-bounded tree. The document highlights the limitations of traditional top-K retrieval and isolated long-context processing, and demonstrates the effectiveness of SPD-RAG in mitigating these limitations. The system is evaluated on the Loong benchmark, where it achieves an average score of 58.1, outperforming Normal RAG and Agentic RAG by approximately 25 absolute points. The document also discusses the latency overhead, limitations, and future work of SPD-RAG, including its potential applications to larger corpora and its scalability properties. Additionally, the document touches on the ethics of using LLM-based systems, including the potential for factual errors and increased energy consumption.

structure.title: Ethics Statement
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the importance of how information is processed for complex information-seeking queries over large corpora. It highlights the effectiveness of ensuring each document receives exhaustive, dedicated agentic attention, rather than simply expanding the raw context a single model can consume. The document also touches on the ethics of using large language models (LLMs) and the need for human verification of outputs. Additionally, it references various studies and benchmarks related to LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and multi-agent systems. The appendix includes prompts for a coordinator and a document sub-agent, outlining their roles and responsibilities in a RAG-based analysis. Overall, the document focuses on the development and evaluation of systems for improving access to information in large document collections.

structure.title: Implementation Details
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a hierarchical multi-agent framework called SPD-RAG, designed for exhaustive multi-document question answering. The system assigns a dedicated agentic RAG module to each document and merges their findings through a similarity-ordered, token-bounded tree. The document covers the methodology, experimental setup, and results of evaluating SPD-RAG on the Loong benchmark, which demonstrates its effectiveness in mitigating the limitations of traditional top-K retrieval and isolated long-context processing. The system achieves an average score of 58.1, outperforming Normal RAG and Agentic RAG by approximately 25 absolute points, while operating at only 37.9% of the API cost of the oracle full-context baseline. The document also discusses the cost-quality tradeoff, analysis by task type and document domain, and limitations of the system. Overall, the partial document presents a novel approach to multi-document question answering and evaluates its performance on a challenging benchmark.
